ORIGINALITY.justification: The work appears to be one of the first systematic studies of transfer learning for reading comprehension: pre-training an AS Reader on large cloze-style corpora (BookTest, CNN/Daily Mail) and evaluating zero-shot and few-shot fine-tuning on bAbI and a SQuAD subset. It clearly differentiates its contributions (showing poor zero-shot transfer, strong benefits from target adjustment, and ablations isolating embeddings vs. encoder effects). Although it does not propose a new architecture or fundamentally new algorithmic technique, framing and empirically validating cross-dataset pre-training for RC is a notable and informative novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and methodically presented—the experimental design (zero-shot evaluation, target adjustment/fine-tuning, and ablations on embeddings vs encoders) and datasets are clearly described, and main claims are stated early. Writing quality is noted as good, related work coverage is thorough, and experimental reporting is precise (accuracy, means/std, p-values, hyperparameters reported). Presentation loses a top score because the summary notes architectural comparability issues (which could introduce some ambiguity in interpretation) and the absence of code is not mentioned, suggesting minor clarity/replicability gaps.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s primary claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by experimental evidence: zero-shot transfer from BookTest/CNN-DM to bAbI is poor, pre-training substantially improves performance after small-sample fine-tuning, and ablations show contributions from both embeddings and encoder parameters. The experimental methodology seems sound (appropriate baselines such as random and most-frequent, multiple runs with means/SDs, one-sided p-values, and reported hyperparameters), and the encoder/embedding ablation is a suitable control. Minor limitations remain—comparisons to task-specific state-of-the-art are constrained by architectural differences (AS Reader processes full context differently) and the model is not competitive on the SQuAD subset—so I judge the paper technically reliable overall but with small caveats that do not undermine the main conclusions.

main_claims: Zero-shot transfer from large cloze-style datasets (BookTest, CNN/Daily Mail) to target reading-comprehension tasks (bAbI) is very poor without any target-domain training.
Pre-training on large-scale cloze-style datasets significantly improves performance when models are fine-tuned on small samples of target-task data (target adjustment).

The benefits of pre-training are present in both model components: pre-trained word embeddings and pre-trained context-encoder (GRU) parameters, with fully pre-trained models outperforming partially pre-trained variants.
This work is, to the authors' knowledge, the first study of transfer learning in reading comprehension.
_summary.methodology: They pre-trained the Attention Sum Reader (AS Reader) on large cloze-style datasets (BookTest and CNN/Daily Mail), evaluated zero-shot transfer to selected bAbI tasks, then performed target adjustment by fine-tuning pre-trained and randomly-initialized models on varying small subsets of bAbI and a single-word-answer subset of SQuAD; they also ablated pre-training to embeddings-only and encoders-only to assess contribution.
baselines: Random baseline (uniformly random word from context)
metrics: Mean and standard deviation across runs
limitations: Zero-shot transfer from the two large cloze-style datasets to target tasks is surprisingly poor.
The AS Reader performance on the SQuAD subset is not competitive with state-of-the-art task-specific models (e.g., DCR).

Architectural differences (e.g., AS Reader processing whole context as one sequence vs. other models splitting context into sentences) affect comparability and may disadvantage the AS Reader in some settings.
